08 Performance — Measure First, Optimize Second

← Back to lessons

The fastest code is code that doesn't run. But the second-fastest is code you don't optimize prematurely. This lesson teaches you to measure performance, identify actual bottlenecks, and apply the right optimization for each problem.

The Performance Mindset

Most React performance advice is wrong — not because the techniques don't work, but because they're applied without measurement. Wrapping everything in React.memo is like putting a turbocharger on a bicycle: technically it's an improvement, but the overhead of carrying the turbocharger slows you down more than the boost helps.

The journalism parallel: A good editor doesn't rewrite every sentence. They identify which paragraphs are weak and fix those. Performance optimization works the same way. Profile your app, find the 2-3 components that are actually slow, fix those. Leave everything else alone. The 80/20 rule applies aggressively here.

The rendering cost model

A React re-render does two things:

  1. Calls your component function — executes the function body, computes JSX
  2. Reconciles the output — diffs against previous output to find DOM changes

For most components, both steps are trivially fast (< 1ms). A re-render becomes expensive when:

  • The function body does heavy computation (sorting thousands of items, parsing data)
  • The component renders a massive tree of children (thousands of DOM nodes)
  • The re-render triggers expensive effects (network requests, DOM measurements)

React.memo: When It Helps (and When It Hurts)

React.memo wraps a component to skip re-rendering when its props haven't changed. It compares new props against old props using shallow equality (Object.is for each prop).

// React.memo in action
const ArticleCard = memo(function ArticleCard({ article }: Props) {
  return (
    <div className="card">
      <h3>{article.title}</h3>
      <p>{article.excerpt}</p>
    </div>
  );
});

// When parent re-renders:
// - If `article` is the same reference → memo skips render
// - If `article` is a new object (even with same values) → renders anyway

When memo helps

  • Component renders a large subtree and props rarely change
  • Component does expensive computation during render
  • Parent re-renders frequently but passes the same props to this child

When memo hurts

  • Props change on every render (new objects, inline functions) — memo does the comparison AND the render
  • Component is cheap to render — the comparison overhead exceeds the render savings
  • You haven't profiled and don't know if this component is a bottleneck

The comparison isn't free. React.memo performs Object.is() on every prop on every render. For a component with 10 props, that's 10 comparisons. If the component usually re-renders anyway (because props usually change), you're doing 10 comparisons plus the full render. Worse than no memo at all.

useMemo and useCallback: Stabilizing References

useMemo caches a computed value. useCallback caches a function reference. Both are about referential stability, not computation speed (usually).

// useMemo: cache an expensive computation
function DataTable({ records }: Props) {
  const [sortField, setSortField] = useState('date');

  // Without useMemo: sorts 10,000 records on every keystroke
  // With useMemo: only re-sorts when records or sortField change
  const sorted = useMemo(
    () => [...records].sort((a, b) => compare(a[sortField], b[sortField])),
    [records, sortField]
  );

  return <Table data={sorted} />;
}

// useCallback: stable function reference for memo'd children
function ArticleList({ articles }: Props) {
  // Without useCallback: new function every render → breaks child memo
  // With useCallback: same reference until articles changes
  const handleDelete = useCallback(
    (id: string) => deleteArticle(id),
    []
  );

  return articles.map(a => (
    <MemoizedCard key={a.id} article={a} onDelete={handleDelete} />
  ));
}
When to useMemo
// YES: Expensive computation
const filtered = useMemo(() =>
  data.filter(expensiveFilter),
  [data]
);

// YES: Referential stability for context
const ctx = useMemo(
  () => ({ user, logout }),
  [user, logout]
);

// NO: Simple computation
const fullName = useMemo(
  () => `${first} ${last}`,
  [first, last]
); // String concat is faster than useMemo
When to useCallback
// YES: Passed to memo'd child
const onClick = useCallback(() => {
  doSomething(id);
}, [id]);
<MemoChild onClick={onClick} />

// YES: Used in useEffect deps
const fetchData = useCallback(() => {
  return api.get(url);
}, [url]);

// NO: Not passed to memo'd child
const handleClick = useCallback(() => {
  setOpen(!open);
}, [open]); // No consumer cares about ref

The rule of thumb: useMemo and useCallback are useful when they prevent downstream work — either skipping a memo'd child's render, or avoiding an effect re-run. If nobody downstream checks the reference, memoizing is pure overhead. The React team has said they may auto-memoize everything in the future (React Compiler / React Forget), which would make manual memoization unnecessary.

React Compiler (React Forget): The Future

The React team is building a compiler that automatically inserts useMemo and useCallback where beneficial. It analyzes your component code at build time and adds memoization that a human would struggle to optimize correctly.

// What you write:
function ArticleCard({ article, onLike }) {
  const formatted = formatDate(article.date);
  const handleClick = () => onLike(article.id);

  return (
    <div>
      <h3>{article.title}</h3>
      <time>{formatted}</time>
      <button onClick={handleClick}>Like</button>
    </div>
  );
}

// What the compiler generates (conceptual):
function ArticleCard({ article, onLike }) {
  const formatted = useMemo(() => formatDate(article.date), [article.date]);
  const handleClick = useCallback(() => onLike(article.id), [onLike, article.id]);

  return useMemo(() => (
    <div>
      <h3>{article.title}</h3>
      <time>{formatted}</time>
      <button onClick={handleClick}>Like</button>
    </div>
  ), [article.title, formatted, handleClick]);
}

This doesn't mean "never optimize manually." The compiler handles memoization, but it can't fix algorithmic problems. If you're rendering 10,000 items without virtualization, or computing a Fibonacci sequence in render, the compiler won't save you. It handles the tedious reference-stability work; you handle the architecture.

Code Splitting: Send Less JavaScript

The most impactful performance optimization is usually sending less code to the browser. Code splitting breaks your bundle into chunks loaded on demand.

// Dynamic import with React.lazy
const HeavyChart = lazy(() => import('./HeavyChart'));

function Dashboard() {
  const [showChart, setShowChart] = useState(false);

  return (
    <div>
      <button onClick={() => setShowChart(true)}>Show Analytics</button>
      {showChart && (
        <Suspense fallback={<ChartSkeleton />}>
          <HeavyChart />  {/* Loaded only when shown */}
        </Suspense>
      )}
    </div>
  );
}

// Next.js dynamic import (with SSR disabled for client-only libs)
import dynamic from 'next/dynamic';

const D3Map = dynamic(() => import('@/components/D3Map'), {
  ssr: false,     // Don't render on server (D3 needs the DOM)
  loading: () => <MapSkeleton />,
});

Route-based splitting (automatic in Next.js)

Next.js automatically code-splits by route. Each page's JavaScript is only loaded when the user navigates to that page. You get this for free — no configuration needed.

Bundle Analysis: Know What You're Shipping

// Install and run bundle analyzer
npm install @next/bundle-analyzer

// next.config.js
const withBundleAnalyzer = require('@next/bundle-analyzer')({
  enabled: process.env.ANALYZE === 'true',
});

module.exports = withBundleAnalyzer({ /* your config */ });

// Run:
ANALYZE=true npm run build

The analyzer produces a visual treemap showing every module in your bundles. Look for:

  • Huge libraries you only use a small part of (moment.js, lodash without tree-shaking)
  • Duplicate packages (two versions of the same library)
  • Client-side code that should be server-only

The React Profiler: Measure Before You Optimize

React DevTools Profiler records every render and shows you exactly which components rendered, how long each took, and why.

How to use the Profiler

  1. Open React DevTools → Profiler tab
  2. Click Record
  3. Interact with your app (type, click, navigate)
  4. Click Stop
  5. Examine the flame chart — each bar is a component render

What to look for

  • Gray bars: Component did not re-render (already optimized)
  • Green/yellow/red bars: Render duration (green = fast, red = slow)
  • "Why did this render?" panel: shows the reason (state change, parent re-rendered, context changed)
// Programmatic profiling for production monitoring
import { Profiler } from 'react';

function onRender(
  id: string,
  phase: 'mount' | 'update',
  actualDuration: number,
) {
  if (actualDuration > 16) { // Slower than one frame (60fps)
    console.warn(`Slow render: ${id} took ${actualDuration}ms`);
    // Send to analytics in production
  }
}

<Profiler id="ArticleList" onRender={onRender}>
  <ArticleList articles={articles} />
</Profiler>

Virtualization: Render Only What's Visible

If you're rendering 1000+ items in a list, the answer isn't memoization. It's rendering only the 20-30 items visible in the viewport. This is virtualization.

// Using @tanstack/react-virtual
import { useVirtualizer } from '@tanstack/react-virtual';

function VirtualArticleList({ articles }: Props) {
  const parentRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);

  const virtualizer = useVirtualizer({
    count: articles.length,
    getScrollElement: () => parentRef.current,
    estimateSize: () => 80, // Estimated row height
  });

  return (
    <div ref={parentRef} style={{ height: '600px', overflow: 'auto' }}>
      <div style={{ height: `${virtualizer.getTotalSize()}px`, position: 'relative' }}>
        {virtualizer.getVirtualItems().map(virtualRow => (
          <div
            key={virtualRow.key}
            style={{
              position: 'absolute',
              top: 0,
              transform: `translateY(${virtualRow.start}px)`,
              height: `${virtualRow.size}px`,
              width: '100%',
            }}
          >
            <ArticleRow article={articles[virtualRow.index]} />
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

Virtualization vs memoization: Memoizing 10,000 list items means React still maintains 10,000 fiber nodes and does 10,000 prop comparisons. Virtualization means React only knows about ~30 items at a time. It's not just faster renders — it's fundamentally less work. For any list over a few hundred items, virtualization beats memoization every time.

Check Your Understanding

1. When does React.memo cause worse performance than no memo?

When props change on most renders. Memo adds a shallow comparison of all props on every render. If the comparison usually finds different props and renders anyway, you're doing the comparison AND the render — more work than just rendering.

2. What is useCallback actually for?

Referential stability. It returns the same function reference between renders (as long as dependencies don't change). This is only useful if the function is passed as a prop to a memo'd child, used in a useEffect dependency array, or used in a useMemo dependency array. Otherwise, it's pure overhead.

3. You have a list of 5,000 articles. Users complain it's slow. What should you try first?

Virtualization (render only visible items). Not React.memo on each item, not useMemo on the list, not moving state. With 5,000 items, the problem is too many DOM nodes and fiber nodes. Virtualization reduces this to ~30 regardless of total list size.

4. What's the first thing you should do when someone reports a performance problem?

Profile. Open React DevTools Profiler, reproduce the slow interaction, and look at the flame chart. Identify which specific components are slow and why. Guessing at optimizations wastes time and often makes things worse.

Exercise: Profile and Optimize

  1. Build a list that renders 2,000 items. Profile it. Note the render time.
  2. Add React.memo to each list item. Profile again. Compare.
  3. Replace memo with virtualization (TanStack Virtual). Profile again. Compare all three.
  4. Run ANALYZE=true npm run build on a project. Find the largest modules. Can any be lazy-loaded or replaced?
← Lesson 07: Data Fetching Lesson 09: Forms & Validation →
